source: public web (Google Trends, Reddit, TikTok, news)

trends.title: Oddly-Satisfying Floor & Surface Reveal Videos
trends.summary: CleanTok continues to be driven by slow-pan before/after 'reveal' clips of grimy floors, stovetops and grout being restored to shine, a durable format that keeps generating high completion rates and shares.
trends.angle: Seed creator content around Swiffer WetJet/Sweeper and Mr. Clean Magic Eraser 'reveal' shots to ride the always-on satisfying-clean format rather than a single viral moment.
trends.response: Brief 3-5 CleanTok creators to film slow-pan before/after floor and stovetop reveals using Swiffer WetJet and Mr. Clean Magic Eraser, tagged #cleantok, and boost top performers as paid.

examples.appeal: The hashtag hub itself shows the reveal format dominating: dramatic dirty-to-clean pans with satisfying music stings drive high watch-through and share rates.
Search results skew toward tight before/after cuts on grout and vinyl flooring, a format that's easy for any home creator to replicate, fueling constant fresh supply.
Reels under this tag pair upbeat audio with quick transformation cuts, a format IG's algorithm favors for Explore-page distribution.

briefs.influencer:
# CREATOR/INFLUENCER BRIEF

## Objective
Ride the always-on "oddly-satisfying reveal" format on CleanTok to drive organic reach, saves, and shares for Swiffer and Mr. Clean by seeding steady, native creator content — not chasing a single viral spike.

## Which P&G brand
Swiffer (WetJet/Sweeper) + Mr. Clean (Magic Eraser)

## Target creators
- CleanTok / satisfying-content creators (10K–500K followers; micro > mega for authenticity)
- Home cleaning, mom/dad-life, and "restoration reveal" niches
- Creators with proven slow-pan/before-after format history and high completion rates
- Mix of 70% micro-creators (seeding volume) / 30% mid-tier (amplification)

## Key message
Real grime, real shine, no gimmicks — Swiffer and Mr. Clean deliver the satisfying reveal moment, every time, on the surfaces people actually struggle with.

## Hook / opening
Open on the grimiest, most relatable dirty surface (stovetop grease ring, grout lines, sticky floor corner) — no context, just the mess — then slow pan/wipe reveal. First 1.5 seconds must show the "before" in full disgusting glory.

## Do
- Use natural lighting, slow deliberate camera pans (no cuts mid-wipe)
- Show full product-in-hand application (WetJet glide / Magic Eraser scrub)
- Let the ASMR sound (squeak, wipe swish) play through — minimal music overlay
- Feature real, lived-in messes (kitchen, bathroom, entryway)
- Keep videos 15–30 sec, vertical, silent-scroll friendly with captions

## Don't
- Don't over-produce or use stock-clean "fake" grime
- Don't rush the reveal — no fast cuts killing the payoff
- Don't over-script product callouts; let visuals sell it
- Don't disparage competitor products by name
- Don't add unrelated trends/audio that distract from the reveal

## Suggested content
1. **Stovetop rescue**: Magic Eraser cutting through burnt grease, close-up shine reveal
2. **Grout line glow-up**: side-by-side before/after grout restoration
3. **WetJet glide-through**: slow-mo floor streaks disappearing in one pass
4. **"Satisfying Sunday reset"** series: multi-surface reveal montage (stove → floor → baseboards)
5. **POV duet/stitch bait**: creator reacts to their own past "before" footage

## Hashtags & CTA
**Hashtags:** #CleanTok #SatisfyingClean #SwifferReveal #MrCleanMagic #CleanReset #ThatShine
**CTA:** "Duet your dirtiest surface — tag us for a reshare" / "Which reveal hit hardest? Drop a 🫧"

## KPI
- Completion rate ≥65%
- Share/save rate benchmark vs. category avg (+20%)
- Comment sentiment (positive/satisfying mentions)
- UGC duet/stitch volume
- Branded hashtag view growth (week-over-week)
briefs.post:
## Platform & format
TikTok + IG Reels — slow-pan before/after reveal, 9:16, native CleanTok editing style (no fancy transitions, just the drag-and-shine cut).

## Caption (ready to post)
The floor was not giving. Then the WetJet said "hold my mop." 🫧✨ Swipe-worthy shine, zero elbow grease. Tag someone who needs this reveal in their life.

## Hook (first line / first 2s)
"POV: you haven't mopped since... we're not gonna talk about it."

## Visual direction
- Open tight on the grimiest section of floor/stove — bad lighting, real grime, no staging.
- Slow diagonal pan as Swiffer WetJet (or Mr. Clean Magic Eraser for stovetop/grout) glides through, wet streak trailing behind.
- Hold 2 sec on the "half clean half dirty" line shot — this is the money frame, let it breathe.
- Final reveal: full shine, natural light hitting the surface, maybe a satisfying squeak sound or ASMR audio.
- Keep it one continuous take if possible — cuts kill the satisfaction.

## Hashtags
#CleanTok #SatisfyingClean #SwifferWetJet #MrCleanMagicEraser #BeforeAndAfter #CleanWithMe #PandG

## CTA
Duet your dirtiest floor/stove and let the WetJet or Magic Eraser do the talking. Best reveal gets reposted 👀🧼

trends.title: Dawn Powerwash Used as All-Purpose Spray Cleaner
trends.summary: Creators keep repurposing Dawn Powerwash spray beyond dishes — on ovens, grout, and outdoor furniture — as a 'one bottle does everything' cleaning hack, extending the product's original grease-cutting reputation.
trends.angle: Lean into consumer-driven multi-surface use cases with official 'approved hacks' content rather than only dish-specific messaging, capturing incremental use occasions.
trends.response: Publish a short 'Dawn Powerwash, more than dishes' Reel/TikTok series showing sanctioned multi-surface uses (oven glass, grill grates) and reply/duet top organic hack videos from Dawn's official account.

examples.appeal: Quick-hit clips showing grease vanishing off ovens/pans in seconds, a visually clear payoff that drives saves and comments asking 'does it really work.'
Reels combine product close-ups with rapid wipe-down transformations, easy to screenshot as a shopping reminder.
Broader dish-soap hack search shows Dawn recurring as the go-to brand named in comments, indicating strong unaided brand association.

briefs.influencer:
# Dawn Powerwash Multi-Use Hack — Creator Brief

## Objective
Capture and validate the organic "Dawn Powerwash as all-purpose cleaner" trend with official brand-backed content, driving incremental use occasions beyond dishes while protecting product safety perception (surface-appropriate use).

## Which P&G brand
Dawn (Powerwash line)

## Target creators
- Cleaning/organization creators (mid-tier, 50K–500K) known for "hack" or "tested it" formats
- Home hack & lifestyle creators with strong before/after editing skills
- A few larger anchor creators (500K+) for reach/credibility
- Mix of clean-freak niche + everyday parent/home creators for relatability

## Key message
"One bottle, way more than dishes." Dawn Powerwash's grease-cutting foam works as a legit multi-surface cleaner — ovens, stovetops, grout, patio furniture — officially tested and approved, not just a viral rumor.

## Hook / opening
"Wait, you're STILL only using Dawn Powerwash on dishes?" or "I let the internet's favorite hack test itself — here's what actually works."

## Do
- Show real before/after on approved surfaces (stovetops, sealed grout, glass, outdoor furniture, sinks)
- Reference the trend explicitly ("saw this all over TikTok, had to test it")
- Include a quick safety/usage caveat (non-porous, sealed, or hard surfaces; spot-test first)
- Keep it visual: spray foam action + wipe-down satisfaction
- Tag @Dawn and use branded hashtag

## Don't
- Use on unsealed wood, electronics, fabric, or delicate finishes
- Imply it replaces dedicated cleaners (oven cleaner, wood polish) entirely
- Overclaim disinfecting or deep-sanitizing power
- Skip the caveat/disclaimer — avoid liability and misuse

## Suggested content
- "Testing the viral Dawn Powerwash hack" multi-surface montage (TikTok)
- "5 things Dawn Powerwash can clean besides dishes" carousel (Instagram)
- Duet/stitch response to top trending hack videos with brand co-sign
- Before/after Reel with satisfying foam-wipe transitions

## Hashtags & CTA
#DawnPowerwash #PowerwashHack #OneBottleWonder
CTA: "Try it & tag @Dawn to get featured" / "Shop Dawn Powerwash — link in bio"

## KPI
- Engagement rate vs. brand benchmark (+20% target)
- Branded hashtag UGC volume
- View-through rate on hack demo content
- Sentiment split (positive vs. safety-concern comments)
- Incremental sales lift / search interest for Powerwash SKU
briefs.post:
# Dawn Powerwash Multi-Use Hack

## Platform & format
TikTok primary (15-20s multi-clip demo) + Instagram Reels crosspost. Carousel version for IG feed with "5 approved hacks" static slides.

## Caption (ready to post)
Ok we saw you using Powerwash on EVERYTHING and… we approve. 👀✨

One bottle. Zero rinse. Endless grease enemies.

You already knew it destroys dishes. Turns out it also handles the oven door, the grill grates, the patio chairs, and that grout you've been avoiding since spring. Same spray-and-wipe formula, just more places to point it.

We're not saying we planned this. We're saying we're not mad about it.

## Hook (first line / first 2s)
"Wait, you're only using this on dishes?"

## Visual direction
Fast cuts, satisfying grease-melt close-ups, quick-hit format:
1. Spray on baked-on oven grime → wipe → clean streak reveal
2. Spray on grout lines → scrub with brush → bright white grout
3. Spray on outdoor patio table (grease/pollen buildup) → wipe → shine
4. End on original use case: dishes, sponge, suds — full circle
Text overlay per clip: "dishes ✅ / oven ✅ / grout ✅ / patio ✅"
Bright kitchen lighting, no music breaks — trending upbeat audio, quick zooms on reveal moments.

## Hashtags
#DawnPowerwash #PowerwashHack #CleanTok #GreaseGone #DawnApproved #MultiUseMagic

## CTA
Drop your Powerwash hack below 👇 we might just repost it (and yes, we're taking notes for the next bottle design).

trends.title: Recurring 'Laundry & Bath Stripping' Deep-Clean Trend
trends.summary: The multi-year 'stripping' trend — soaking laundry, bath mats or blinds in hot water with detergent, borax and soda to pull out grime — keeps resurfacing seasonally (notably around fall deep-cleans) even after its 2021-22 peak.
trends.angle: The trend generates real safety and surface-damage concerns (mixing cleaning chemicals, overloading washers), giving P&G Home Care an opening to publish a safer, branded 'deep clean reset' routine ahead of the holiday hosting season.
trends.response: Publish an educational short-form video positioning a safe P&G-approved seasonal deep-clean routine (Cascade/Dawn/Swiffer combo) as a safer, equally satisfying alternative to DIY 'stripping,' and flag chemical-mixing risk in comments on trending stripping videos.

examples.appeal: The murky 'strip water' reveal shot is the hook — viewers are shocked at how dirty 'clean' laundry looks, driving high shares and disbelief comments.
Bathroom-mat version of the trend reuses the same shock-value dirty-water reveal, keeping the format fresh across new household items.
Reels frame stripping as part of a broader seasonal 'deep clean reset,' tapping into fall/winter nesting motivation.

briefs.influencer:
## Objective
Redirect "laundry/bath stripping" trend energy into a safer, brand-led "Deep Clean Reset" routine ahead of holiday hosting — reducing DIY chemical-mixing risk while driving product trial.

## Which P&G brand
Mr. Clean (lead, deep-clean authority) + Dawn (grease-cutting co-star for the soak step). Optional Febreze tie-in for "reset" freshness finish.

## Target creators
- Cleaning/organizing creators (50K–500K followers) known for satisfying "stripping" or deep-clean content
- Home hacks & budget-cleaning micro-influencers (10K–100K) — high trust, high engagement
- 1–2 safety-savvy creators (nurses, chemists, "clean with caution" niche) to reinforce credibility
- Avoid mega-celebrity/lifestyle creators with no cleaning credibility

## Key message
"Skip the guesswork and the hazardous mix — get the same deep-clean satisfaction with a safe, simple 2-product routine designed to actually work."

## Hook / opening
"Everyone's 'stripping' their laundry with borax and bleach mixed together... please don't. Here's the dirty water effect WITHOUT the chemical risk." (Show grimy soak water reveal — the visual payoff people love — but from a safe formula.)

## Do
- Show real, visible "dirty water" payoff (the trend's core appeal) using Mr. Clean + Dawn only
- Call out specific unsafe combos (bleach + ammonia/borax) factually, not fear-mongering
- Frame as "reset," not "one-time hack" — tie to recurring seasonal routine (fall/holiday hosting prep)
- Include washer-safe load size / soak time guidance on-screen
- Post-clean freshness beat (Febreze) for sensory close

## Don't
- Don't name-check or disparage specific competitor brands or creators who've posted the original trend
- Don't show soda crystals/borax at all, even as "before" comparison — avoid legitimizing the mix
- Don't overclaim ("kills all germs," "removes years of buildup") — keep claims product-approved
- Don't skip safety disclaimers (ventilation, no mixing chemicals, patch test on surfaces)

## Suggested content
1. **"Stripping Swap" duet/stitch** — react to a viral stripping video, redo it the safe way
2. **Before/after soak reveal** — bath mat or towel, hot water + Mr. Clean, dirty water payoff shot
3. **"Holiday Hosting Reset" checklist carousel** (IG/FB) — 5-step deep clean using Mr. Clean, Dawn, Febreze
4. **POV: Reddit/TikTok made me do it** — myth-busting caption format, casual tone, not preachy

## Hashtags & CTA
#DeepCleanReset #CleanSafely #MrCleanMagic #StripSafely (owned alt-hashtag)
CTA: "Try the safe stripping swap before your next guest arrives — link in bio for the full routine."

## KPI
- Engagement rate ≥ trend benchmark (target 6%+)
- Share/save rate on safety-callout content (proxy for trust-building)
- Branded hashtag UGC volume (target 500+ posts in 30 days)
- Sentiment shift: track % positive/neutral mentions of P&G brands in stripping-adjacent conversation pre/post campaign
briefs.post:
## Platform & format
TikTok-first vertical video (15-25s), repurposed as IG Reels + FB Video. Text-on-screen "myth vs fact" style with quick cuts.

## Caption (ready to post)
Stripping your laundry with borax, soda AND detergent in one bucket? That's not a deep clean, that's a chemistry experiment. 🧪🚫

Skip the guesswork before hosting season hits. Here's the reset that actually works — no fumes, no ruined washer, no soaked-through bath mat drama.

Grab Mr. Clean for the grime, Dawn for the cut-through-grease soak, and let the pros (us) handle the "deep" part of deep clean.

Your washer — and your lungs — will thank you.

## Hook (first line / first 2s)
"Please stop mixing your cleaning products in a bucket."

## Visual direction
- Open on chaotic "stripping" setup: bucket overflowing with borax, soda, detergent, murky water — text overlay "DIY stripping trend rn"
- Quick record-scratch freeze, cut to clean, bright kitchen/bathroom
- Split-screen: LEFT = grimy bath mat soak gone wrong (overflowing tub, fumes emoji graphic) vs RIGHT = simple Mr. Clean + Dawn routine (mop, soak bin, one product at a time)
- Close-ups of product labels, sudsy-but-controlled soak, mat coming out fresh
- End card: "The Deep Clean Reset" with Mr. Clean + Dawn bottles, holiday-ready home shot (guests walking in, candles, cozy vibes)

## Hashtags
#DeepCleanReset #MrClean #CleaningTok #LaundryHacks #StrippingTrend #HostingSeasonReady #CleanSafely #DawnDish

## CTA
Save this before your next "deep clean Sunday" — and tag someone who still has borax in their cart. Comment "RESET" for our full safe stripping-swap routine.

trends.title: Scent-Layering & Odor Elimination Routines Trending
trends.summary: A broader home-fragrance culture — layering candles, plug-ins and fabric sprays for a signature 'house smell' — keeps driving CleanTok/HomeTok content, with odor-elimination call-outs (pets, cooking smells) as a recurring sub-theme.
trends.angle: Position Febreze as the foundational 'odor elimination' layer beneath decorative scent products in creator routines, reinforcing it as a function-first complement rather than competing purely on fragrance.
trends.response: Seed HomeTok creators to feature Febreze Fabric Refresher as step one in 'scent layering' routine videos (odor elimination before fragrance), and run a branded sound/challenge around 'what does your house smell like.'

examples.appeal: Routine-style videos walking through each scent product used room-by-room perform well because they double as shoppable haul content.
Aesthetic flat-lay Reels of candles and sprays pair aspirational home decor with practical product recommendations, driving saves.
Search results show creators using Febreze specifically for odor call-outs (pet smell, gym bags), reinforcing its trusted function-first reputation.

briefs.influencer:
## Objective
Ride the Scent-Layering/HomeTok trend by repositioning Febreze as the essential *base layer* of any signature-scent routine — the odor-elimination step that makes candles, plug-ins, and sprays actually smell good, rather than a competitor to them.

## Which P&G brand
Febreze (Fabric Refresher, Air Heavy Duty, Small Spaces)

## Target creators
- CleanTok/HomeTok creators (50K–500K followers) known for "get ready with me" cleaning or room-refresh routines
- Pet-owner lifestyle creators (odor callout angle)
- Home decor/candle-layering creators who already build "signature house scent" content
- Micro-creators (10-50K) in apartment/small-space niche for relatable odor problems (cooking, pets, gym bags)

## Key message
"Layer smarter: eliminate first, then decorate." Febreze removes the odor molecules that block your candles and plug-ins from smelling their best — it's the invisible first step in every great scent routine, not another fragrance competing for attention.

## Hook / opening
"Your candle isn't the problem — what's under it is." / "POV: you've been scent-layering wrong this whole time" / "Step 1 of my signature house smell routine (it's not a candle)"

## Do
- Show Febreze applied *first* — fabrics, carpets, air — before candles/plug-ins/sprays are introduced
- Use real trigger scenarios: pet beds, kitchen after cooking, gym bag, couch cushions
- Frame Febreze as "the reset button" or "clean canvas" language
- Pair with at least one other home-fragrance brand/product to show complementary layering, not competition
- Include quick before/after or "sniff test" moments (visual/audio cues, not just claims)
- Keep tone light, aspirational, ASMR-adjacent (satisfying spray sounds, folding fabric, etc.)

## Don't
- Don't position Febreze as a decorative scent or "signature smell" itself
- Don't disparage candles/plug-ins/other fragrance brands
- Don't overclaim medical/allergen elimination — stick to "odor elimination," not sanitization claims
- Don't skip the odor-source demonstration (pet, cooking, laundry) — abstract "freshness" claims underperform
- Don't make it feel like a traditional ad read; keep native routine format

## Suggested content
1. **"My Layering Routine" TikTok**: Febreze fabric/air first → plug-in → candle, narrated as a 3-step ritual
2. **Pet-owner Reel**: "Foster failed again, here's how I keep the house guest-ready" — Febreze on bedding/furniture as step 1
3. **Cooking-smell transformation**: Post-fry/fish odor eliminated with Febreze before lighting a candle for ambiance
4. **"Things in my small apartment that changed my scent game"** listicle format, Febreze as unsexy-but-essential #1

## Hashtags & CTA
**Hashtags:** #ScentLayering #HouseSmell #FebrezeFirst #HomeTok #CleanTok #OdorElimination #SignatureScent
**CTA:** "Tag the candle/plug-in you layer with Febreze 👇" or "Duet your layering routine — start with the reset step"

## KPI
- Video completion rate & saves (routine content = high save intent)
- Comment sentiment mentioning "didn't know that's what Febreze was for" / reframing awareness
- Branded hashtag UGC volume (
briefs.post:
## Platform & format
TikTok primary (15-20s trending audio + text overlay routine video), repurposed as Instagram Reel. Bonus static carousel for IG feed: "The Scent Layering Pyramid."

## Caption (ready to post)
Your candle isn't doing what you think it's doing. 🕯️🚫

Layering scents only works if there's nothing left to cover up. That's step zero.

Febreze eliminates the odor. Then your candles, plug-ins, and room sprays get to actually shine (instead of fighting last night's salmon).

Build your signature house smell on a clean canvas. 🏡✨

## Hook (first line / first 2s)
"POV: you've bought 47 candles and your house still smells like the dog."

## Visual direction
- Open on a chaotic "scent shelf" — candles, plug-ins, sprays lined up, slightly messy, relatable
- Quick-cut montage: creator spritzing Febreze on couch/curtains/pet bed FIRST
- Text overlay: "Step 1: Eliminate" → beat drop → "Step 2: Layer" (candle lit, plug-in clicked in, fabric spray)
- End on cozy, styled room with soft lighting — "after" shot feels aspirational, not clinical
- Consider split-screen: left = candle alone (still smells like dinner), right = Febreze + candle (clean bliss)

## Hashtags
#HomeTok #CleanTok #ScentLayering #FebrezeIt #OdorFree #HouseSmell #CleaningRoutine #SignatureScent

## CTA
Drop your scent-layering stack below 👇 — we'll tell you where Febreze fits in.

trends.title: Satisfying scrub-and-shine cleaning duo still trending
trends.summary: The Pink Stuff paste and Scrub Daddy sponge continue to anchor the 'oddly satisfying' cleaning genre on TikTok, with creators pairing the two for before/after grime-removal transformations on stovetops, pots, and grout.
trends.angle: The core appeal is the transformation/ASMR scrub reveal, not brand loyalty — Mr. Clean and Dawn can compete on the same satisfying-clean-reveal format rather than product superiority claims.
trends.response: Seed a side-by-side 'satisfying reveal' duet series with Mr. Clean Magic Eraser and Dawn Powerwash on grimy stovetops/pans, using the same slow-pan lighting and ASMR scrub sound design that makes Pink Stuff/Scrub Daddy videos shareable.

examples.appeal: Slow-motion before/after wipe reveals rely on ASMR scrubbing sound and a satisfying 'clean line' visual that drives shares and comments.
The sponge's color-changing texture (soft in warm water, firm in cold) is a built-in visual gimmick that creators show off in quick demo clips.
Reels compilations of grimy oven/pan transformations get high save-rates because viewers screenshot for their own cleaning day.

briefs.influencer:
## Objective
Ride the "scrub-and-shine" satisfying-clean trend without chasing The Pink Stuff/Scrub Daddy directly — win the format (grime-to-shine transformation, ASMR scrub audio, dramatic before/after) with Mr. Clean and Dawn, not a superiority claim.

## Which P&G brand
Mr. Clean (Magic Eraser + Multi-Surface) as lead; Dawn Powerwash as secondary for pots/pans/greasy stovetop cuts.

## Target creators
- Mid-tier cleaning/satisfying-content creators (50K–500K), especially grout, oven, stovetop, and pot-scrub niches
- ASMR cleaning accounts (soft-spoken or sound-only formats)
- Home organization/deep-clean creators who already do before/after reveals
- Avoid mega-influencers with no cleaning niche credibility — authenticity of "grime" matters more than reach here

## Key message
The reveal is the reward. Mr. Clean Magic Eraser and Dawn Powerwash deliver the same jaw-dropping grime-to-shine transformation this trend is built on — let the product do the talking through the reveal, not a claim.

## Hook / opening
Cold open on the dirtiest, grimiest surface possible (crusted stovetop, blackened pot, grungy grout) — no face, no intro — just "wait for it" energy. First 2 seconds = worst-case grime. Cut hard to shine.

## Do
- Lead with sound design: scrubbing, sizzle-fizz, squeak-clean audio front and center
- Use a single continuous shot or match-cut transformation (no jump cuts that feel edited/fake)
- Show the product in-hand mid-scrub, not just as a label shot
- Let comments/duets happen — repost strong UGC reveals
- Keep captions minimal; let visuals/audio carry it

## Don't
- Don't disparage or directly compare to Pink Stuff/Scrub Daddy — no "better than" language
- Don't over-produce with music overlays that bury the ASMR scrub sound
- Don't fake or over-stage grime (baked-on food, actual rust) — must read as real
- Don't force brand mentions more than 1x on-screen; let product visual do the work

## Suggested content
1. **"Worst pot in my house" challenge** — Dawn Powerwash cuts through baked-on grease in one continuous pour-foam-scrub-rinse shot
2. **Grout revival** — Mr. Clean Magic Eraser on filthy tile lines, close-up macro shot of white lines reappearing
3. **Silent scrub series** — no talking, just satisfying sound, captioned "no thoughts, just clean"
4. **Duet-bait** — post an intentionally oddly satisfying reveal and invite creators to duet their own version

## Hashtags & CTA
#SatisfyingClean #CleanTok #BeforeAndAfterClean #MrCleanMagic #DawnPowerwash
CTA: "Show us your worst grime moment — duet this with your own reveal."

## KPI
- 3M+ combined organic views across TikTok/IG/YT in 30 days
- 15%+ engagement rate on branded content (vs. category avg ~8%)
- 50+ organic duets/stitches
- Positive sentiment ≥85% in comments (no brand-bashing backlash)
briefs.post:
## Platform & format
TikTok primary (1080x1920, 15–22s), cross-post to IG Reels + YT Shorts. Satisfying before/after scrub reveal, same genre as Pink Stuff/Scrub Daddy duo videos — just Mr. Clean + Dawn in the lead roles.

## Caption (ready to post)
we don't need a duo when the Magic Eraser and Dawn already run this genre 🫧✨ grease didn't stand a chance. tag someone who needs this reset

## Hook (first line / first 2s)
"Everyone's obsessed with the pink paste combo... okay bet."

## Visual direction
- Open on genuinely disgusting stovetop/pot/grout (real grime, not staged)
- Split-screen or side-swipe transition into the clean
- Dawn suds applied first (foam close-up, satisfying squish sound)
- Mr. Clean Magic Eraser scrub-in, slow motion on the grime lifting off in one wipe
- End on a hard light flash to reveal the shine, mirror-clean shot
- Trending audio: low-fi satisfying ASMR track or the same sound used in Pink Stuff/Scrub Daddy trend videos (jump on it, don't reinvent)
- On-screen text: "same genre, different duo" appears right at the reveal

## Hashtags
#SatisfyingClean #CleanTok #BeforeAndAfter #MagicEraser #DawnDish #OddlySatisfying #CleaningHack #GreaseGone

## CTA
"Drop your grossest before-pic below 👇 we'll find you a duo."

trends.title: DIY hypochlorous-acid 'clean with electricity' trend grows
trends.summary: Interest in electrolyzed water / hypochlorous acid cleaners continues to build as part of the broader 'non-toxic, refillable, make-your-own' cleaning movement, positioned as a chemical-free alternative to bleach-based multi-purpose sprays.
trends.angle: The trend's core promise is 'safe around kids/pets and no harsh chemical smell,' a lane P&G brands can occupy via transparency content and lower-fume/plant-based line messaging rather than ceding the whole 'clean label' conversation.
trends.response: Publish an ingredient-transparency explainer reel from a P&G Home Care scientist demystifying hypochlorous acid vs. traditional cleaners, and spotlight any fragrance-free/sensitive-skin Dawn or Mr. Clean SKUs as the 'no gadget required' alternative.

examples.appeal: The novelty of a countertop device that turns tablets/water into cleaner via electrolysis is inherently demo-able and sparks curiosity comments.
Creators frame it as a 'science hack' reveal, which taps into the broader appeal of DIY/clean-label content for skeptical, ingredient-conscious viewers.
Pastel packaging and refill-tablet visuals fit the aspirational, minimalist 'clean home aesthetic' feed style that performs well as static carousel posts.

briefs.influencer:
## Objective
Ride the hypochlorous-acid / "clean with electricity" DIY wave without ceding the clean-label conversation — position Mr. Clean (and Force of Nature-adjacent messaging) as the trusted, tested, "safe around kids/pets, no harsh fumes" choice vs. risky DIY electrolysis at home.

## Which P&G brand
Mr. Clean (primary — multi-surface authority, "power without harsh fumes" credibility). Febreze as secondary tie-in for odor/air-safety angle if creator content leans lifestyle/home-with-pets.

## Target creators
- Clean-label / "crunchy mom" home creators (50K–500K) who already post DIY cleaner content
- Pet-parent and new-parent lifestyle creators
- Micro science-communicators who can explain hypochlorous acid chemistry credibly
- Home organization/cleaning ASMR creators on TikTok & YouTube Shorts

## Key message
"You don't need a DIY electricity rig to get a safe, low-fume clean — Mr. Clean's [plant-based/lower-fume line] gives you that same peace of mind, tested and ready, no chemistry set required."

## Hook / opening
"Everyone's DIYing hospital-grade cleaner in their kitchen right now... here's what you actually need to know before you try it — and the shortcut that skips the guesswork."

## Do
- Acknowledge the trend respectfully — validate the *why* (fumes, kids, pets, transparency)
- Show ingredient transparency (label read-throughs, "what's NOT in this")
- Demonstrate real-world safe use around kids/pets in b-roll
- Include a factual note on DIY electrolysis limitations (unstable shelf life, inconsistent concentration) — informative, not fear-mongering
- Keep tone: helpful big-sister/scientist, not defensive

## Don't
- Don't disparage or mock DIY creators/community
- Don't make unverified medical/safety claims about homemade HOCl
- Don't over-promise "chemical-free" — say "lower-fume/plant-based" per legal-approved copy
- Don't use fear tactics ("DIY could hurt your kids") — stay factual, not alarmist

## Suggested content
1. "I tried the DIY hypochlorous trend so you don't have to" honest test + pivot to product
2. Ingredient transparency carousel/story: "What's actually in your cleaner"
3. Pet/kid safe-clean routine using product, filmed candidly at home
4. Side-by-side smell/fume test (no harsh chemical smell close-up reaction)
5. Creator Q&A / duet responding to viral DIY electrolysis videos

## Hashtags & CTA
#CleanWithoutCompromise #MrCleanSafe #ForceOfHabit #NoHarshFumes #PetSafeClean
CTA: "Skip the DIY guesswork — link in bio for the low-fume clean that's already tested for your home."

## KPI
- Engagement rate ≥6% on branded content
- Share/save rate (indicates trust/utility signal)
- Sentiment analysis: ≥80% positive/neutral comments
- Trend-hashtag share of voice growth for Mr. Clean vs. competitor clean-label mentions
- Earned media pickup referencing P&G's "transparency" stance
briefs.post:
## Platform & format
TikTok + IG Reels (15-20s side-by-side demo), cross-post to YouTube Shorts.

## Caption (ready to post)
Electrolyzed water is having a moment, and honestly? We love the mindset — less harsh chemical smell, safe around the kids/pets, nothing to hide. That's the same energy behind Mr. Clean's lower-fume formulas. You don't need a science kit and a 9-volt battery to clean without the fumes. Just saying. 🔌🧼

## Hook (first line / first 2s)
"So people are electrocuting water to clean their counters now?"

## Visual direction
Split screen: left side = someone fumbling with electrodes, salt packets, a battery, and a jar labeled "Day 3 of my HOCl experiment" (played for light comedy, not mockery). Right side = one spray of Mr. Clean, quick wipe, clean counter, kid/dog walks by immediately after, no reaction to smell. Text overlay: "the lab vs. the label." End card: product shot + ingredient transparency callout ("no bleach fumes, safe to use around pets once dry").

## Hashtags
#MrClean #CleanTok #NonToxicCleaning #CleaningHacks #ChemicalFree #PetSafeCleaning #CleanWithConfidence

## CTA
Comment "LABEL" and we'll drop the full ingredient breakdown — no beakers required.

trends.title: Fabuloso's signature lavender scent drives loyalty and mix warnings
trends.summary: Fabuloso's purple lavender formula remains a cult-favorite 'scent memory' product on social, with fans repeatedly posting about the smell as a comfort/nostalgia trigger, alongside recurring reminder content about never mixing it with bleach.
trends.angle: Scent-as-nostalgia is the real driver here, not floor-cleaning performance — Swiffer and Mr. Clean can lean into signature-scent storytelling and safe-mixing education to borrow equity from the 'smell of a clean home' conversation.
trends.response: Launch a 'scent memory' UGC prompt for Mr. Clean's classic scent alongside a light-hearted but clear chemical-safety PSA reel (never mix cleaners) to ride the safety-awareness spike without disparaging a specific competitor.

examples.appeal: Nostalgia-driven 'this smell = my grandma's house' hook is instantly relatable and invites thousands of comment replies sharing personal scent memories.
Simple mop/floor before-and-after shine shots paired with the recognizable purple bottle make for quick, easily-remixed content.
Meme-style posts joking about people 'drinking in' the fragrance tap into humor and relatability, boosting shares beyond typical cleaning-content audiences.

briefs.influencer:
# Creator/Influencer Brief

## Objective
Borrow equity from the "Fabuloso scent = nostalgia/comfort" cultural moment without referencing competitor mixing risks negatively — position Mr. Clean and Febreze as the safe, signature-scent home-care heroes that deliver the same emotional payoff ("smell of a clean home") plus real safety credibility.

## Which P&G brand
**Mr. Clean** (lead — direct multi-purpose/floor cleaner category overlap, signature scent equity) + **Febreze** (secondary — scent-memory/nostalgia angle, home-freshness storytelling)

## Target creators
- Home/cleaning creators (cleantok, "clean with me") with 20K–500K followers, high comment engagement
- Nostalgia/lifestyle creators who do "smells of my childhood" or "comfort scent" content
- Latino/multicultural home creators (core Fabuloso fan base) to organically bridge the scent-nostalgia conversation
- Micro cleaning-safety educators (chemistry/mom-safety niche) for credible mixing-education content

## Key message
"The smell of a clean home is a memory-maker — and it should always be a safe one." Scent nostalgia + safety confidence = the real win. Mr. Clean and Febreze let you build that signature-scent ritual without ever worrying about what's underneath.

## Hook / opening
- "The smell that means 'clean' in your house... is it doing double duty as a safety risk?"
- "POV: you finish cleaning and the house smells SO good you just stand in the kitchen for a sec"
- "Scent nostalgia is real. Chemical accidents shouldn't be."

## Do
- Lead with sensory/nostalgic storytelling (childhood home, mom cleaning Saturdays, "that smell = comfort")
- Naturally include a "never mix cleaning products" safety PSA as helpful, not preachy
- Show real cleaning application (floors, counters, textiles) with product visibly in use
- Use warm, personal tone — first-person memory framing performs best
- Tag P&G brand handle + include one clear safety fact (e.g., "bleach + ammonia = toxic fumes")

## Don't
- Do NOT name or disparage Fabuloso or any competitor directly
- Don't make safety messaging feel like a lecture or liability disclaimer
- Don't oversell "performance" claims — this is emotional/scent-driven, not a floor-cleaning efficacy pitch
- No fear-mongering imagery (no closeups of chemical danger/hazard symbols)

## Suggested content
1. **"Scent memory" TikTok/Reel**: creator narrates a core memory tied to a clean-home smell while using Mr. Clean/Febreze, ends on product reveal
2. **Cleaning ASMR + safety tip overlay**: satisfying multi-surface clean video with a quick, friendly on-screen safety fact about never mixing cleaners
3. **Facebook community post**: nostalgic carousel ("smells that mean home") with a caption nodding to safe cleaning habits
4. **Duet/stitch-style response** (organic, not directly targeting Fabuloso content) reacting to "cleaning nostalgia" trend with own brand ritual

## Hashtags & CTA
`#SmellOfClean #CleanHomeHappy #MrClean #Febreze #CleanSafely #ScentMemory`
CTA: "Tag the smell that means 'home' to you 🏡✨ — and always clean smart, not mixed."
briefs.post:
## Platform & format
TikTok primary (15–20s scent-memory + safety PSA hybrid), repurposed as IG Reel + FB video post with captioned text overlay.

## Caption (ready to post)
That smell when you walk into your grandma's house? Yeah, we know the one. 💭 Mr. Clean's got signature scents that hit the same nostalgia button — minus the "wait, can I mix this?" panic. Clean home, clear conscience. Never mix cleaning products with bleach, besties. Safety first, scent second (but the scent is REALLY good).

## Hook (first line / first 2s)
"POV: you open the closet and it smells like *clean* clean." (zoom on Mr. Clean bottle, steam/scent visual FX)

## Visual direction
- Open on a nostalgic home setting (kitchen floor being mopped, sunlight through windows) — warm, memory-core aesthetic.
- Quick cut to hand pouring Mr. Clean into bucket, scent lines/steam graphic rising off the water.
- Text overlay: "the smell = the memory" then transition to "the ingredients matter too 👀"
- End card: split-screen "DO mix with water" ✅ vs "DON'T mix with bleach" ❌ — clean iconography, not preachy, quick and stylish like a PSA meme.
- Close on product shot + soft glow, tagline card.

## Hashtags
#MrClean #ScentMemory #CleanHomeCleanMind #SafeCleaningTips #NostalgiaCore #CleaningTok #HomeScents

## CTA
Comment your favorite "clean home" scent memory 👇 and follow for cleaning tips that actually smell (and work) like magic.

trends.title: Minimalist refill brands fuel 'clean-girl' home aesthetic
trends.summary: Method, Blueland, and Mrs. Meyer's continue to benefit from the aesthetic-minimalism and sustainability wave, with creators decanting cleaners into matching bottles and showcasing refill tablets/pods as part of curated 'clean home' room tours.
trends.angle: The visual/sustainability aesthetic — not efficacy — is what earns shares, so P&G Home Care can compete via refillable formats and design-forward packaging content rather than only performance claims.
trends.response: Seed a 'refill, don't landfill' aesthetic pantry-organization reel featuring any P&G concentrate/refill formats in matching decanted bottles, targeting home-organization and sustainability creators for repost.

examples.appeal: Watching a tablet dissolve into a spray bottle is a satisfying, low-waste 'magic trick' visual that's easy to loop and rewatch.
Color-coordinated pantry/cabinet organization shots tap into the broader home-aesthetic trend, driving saves from viewers wanting the same look.
Seasonal scent launches (e.g., limited-edition fragrances) generate unboxing-style Reels that read as gift-worthy, boosting shareability.

briefs.influencer:
## Objective
Capture share of the "clean-girl" aesthetic-minimalism trend by positioning a P&G Home Care brand as refill-first and shelf-worthy — winning on design/sustainability content, not just efficacy claims.

## Which P&G brand
**Mr. Clean** — Clean Freak refillable concentrate system is the strongest fit: consumers already decant into a reusable spray bottle, making it a natural "match the aesthetic" play against Method/Blueland/Mrs. Meyer's.

## Target creators
- Home organization / "clean home" room-tour creators (50K–500K followers)
- Sustainability-minded lifestyle micro-influencers

- Renters/small-space creators doing "clean girl era" routines

## Key message
"The refill is the ritual." Mr. Clean's concentrate + reusable bottle system lets you build the same curated, minimalist cleaning shelf you love — refill, don't rebuy, and skip the plastic waste.

"POV: your cleaning cabinet finally looks as good as your skincare shelf 🧴✨" or "I switched my cleaning products to refills and my cabinet has never looked better."

- Use soft, natural lighting; muted/neutral color grading

## Don't
- Don't lead with grease-cutting/germ-kill claims — aesthetic first, performance secondary

- Don't make it feel like an ad script — keep tone diary-style/authentic
